items.question_text: To what operations may MARSA be applied?

choices.text: Only to military operations specified in a letter of agreement or other appropriate FAA or military document.
To any mixed civil or military operation whenever the pilot requests it on frequency.
Only to VFR military operations inside special use airspace.
To any aircraft pair once ATC determines MARSA would simplify the sector workload.

items.explanation: MARSA is limited to specified military operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate FAA or military document.

items.question_text: DoD must ensure that military pilots requesting special use airspace or ATCAAs have coordinated with the scheduling agency, obtained approval for entry, and are familiar with the appropriate MARSA procedures.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That obligation belongs to DoD, not to ATC.

items.question_text: If military aircraft are engaged in MARSA operations, ATC must provide separation between those participating aircraft and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft but does not invoke or deny MARSA itself.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: ATC's role is limited to separation from nonparticipating IFR aircraft; ATC does not invoke or deny MARSA.

items.question_text: This ATC responsibility applies only to separation between military aircraft in MARSA operations and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft, not to separation among the participating MARSA aircraft themselves.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Terms of MARSA assign responsibility and provide separation among participating aircraft; ATC's role is with respect to nonparticipating IFR traffic.

items.situation: A military unit says it is invoking MARSA for a mission, but there is no supporting LOA or other appropriate document covering the operation. A controller proposes approving MARSA anyway because all participating aircraft agree, while also expecting ATC to manage separation within the military group.
items.para_context: MARSA may be applied only to specified military operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate FAA or military document. ATC does not invoke or deny MARSA; its sole responsibility is separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA operations and nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Treat MARSA as limited to properly documented military operations and keep ATC focused on separation from nonparticipating IFR aircraft rather than separation within the MARSA group.
Approve MARSA on frequency whenever the pilots agree, and take responsibility for separation within the participating aircraft group.
Deny all future MARSA requests because ATC has final authority to invoke or deny MARSA for every unit.
Allow the military aircraft into special use airspace without regard to whether scheduling coordination or approval for entry occurred.

items.explanation: Documented applicability, military command prerogative, and ATC's limited separation role are the controlling points in the paragraph.

items.question_text: Why are military procedures prescribed as additions, modifications, and exceptions to basic FAA procedure?

choices.text: When a common procedure has not been attained or when needed to fulfill a specific requirement.
Whenever a military airport prefers local wording instead of FAA procedures.
Only when civil traffic is absent from the area.
Whenever a military unit wants to override a standing LOA unilaterally.

items.explanation: Military procedures exist only for those specific reasons, not as a blanket alternative to FAA procedure.

items.question_text: Military procedures must be applied by ATC facilities operated by that military service.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: A facility operated by a military service applies that service's procedures to the traffic it handles.

items.question_text: If an ATC facility, regardless of parent organization, supports a designated military airport exclusively, controllers must apply the military procedures determined by that designation.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Exclusive support of a designated military airport causes the designated military procedures to apply regardless of the facility's parent organization.

items.question_text: This military-procedure rule applies to military-operated facilities, to facilities exclusively supporting a designated military airport, and to other facilities when specified in a letter of agreement.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the three application buckets listed in the paragraph.

items.situation: An FAA facility supports a designated USAF base exclusively. A trainee argues that FAA procedures should still apply because the parent organization is FAA, not the Air Force.
items.para_context: When an ATC facility, regardless of parent organization, supports a designated military airport exclusively, the designation determines which military procedures are applied.

choices.text: Apply the designated military procedures because exclusive support of that military airport controls, not the parent organization's label.
Apply only basic FAA procedures because only military-operated facilities can ever use military procedures.
Apply whichever procedures the first arriving aircraft requests on frequency.
Alternate between FAA and military procedures depending on whether the current aircraft is civil or military.

items.explanation: The paragraph bases the procedure set on the designated support relationship, not just the owner of the facility.

items.question_text: How are formation flights controlled under 2-1-13?

choices.text: As a single aircraft.
As separate aircraft at all times, regardless of formation status.
As a single aircraft only after beacon codes are consolidated.
As separate aircraft during join-up but as a single aircraft only after departure from controlled airspace.

items.explanation: Formation flights are controlled as a single aircraft.

items.question_text: For a standard formation, only the aircraft acting as the lead must squawk the ATC-assigned beacon code, and all other aircraft must squawk standby.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the standard-formation beacon-code rule after join-up.

items.question_text: If two aircraft request a formation join-up, controllers may support the join-up only when requested by a participating pilot, all participating pilots concur, and either participating pilot reports the other in sight.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: All three listed conditions must be met for the two-aircraft join-up support case.

items.question_text: This formation-flight rule places separation responsibility between aircraft within the formation on the flight leader and other formation pilots, including the transition periods during join-up and breakaway.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Internal formation separation remains with the flight leader and participating pilots, including transition periods.

items.situation: A standard formation has completed join-up, and one wing aircraft is still squawking an assigned beacon code instead of standby. Another controller says every aircraft in any formation should keep its individual code because it is safer for the radar display.
items.para_context: After join-up, beacon-code assignment depends on formation type. For a standard formation, only the lead squawks the ATC-assigned beacon code and all other aircraft squawk standby.

choices.text: Have the nonlead aircraft squawk standby so the standard-formation code setup matches the rule.
Leave all aircraft on assigned codes because standard and nonstandard formations use the same beacon-code rule.
Cancel the formation status because beacon-code configuration is solely a pilot choice once join-up is complete.
Assign the wing aircraft a second lead code because standard formations require at least two active squawks.

items.explanation: The code rule turns on formation type, and standard formations use one lead squawk with the rest in standby.

items.question_text: What must be ensured before you allow an aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction?

choices.text: That the necessary coordination has been accomplished.
That the receiving controller has already issued the next clearance directly to the pilot.
That the aircraft has changed frequency even if no coordination has occurred.
That the pilot prefers the route change, even if affected controllers have not been told.

items.explanation: Necessary coordination is completed before allowing entry into another controller's area.

items.question_text: Before issuing a control instruction directly to a pilot that changes heading, route, speed, or altitude, controllers must ensure coordination is completed with all controllers whose areas of jurisdiction are affected unless otherwise specified by an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Affected controllers must be covered by coordination before the direct instruction is issued unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise.

items.question_text: If your control instruction will be relayed to the pilot through an FSS, New York Radio, San Francisco Radio, another pilot, or another non-radar-controller source, you are still responsible for ensuring all required coordination is completed.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Relaying the instruction through another source does not remove the issuing controller's responsibility for coordination.

items.question_text: This coordination rule applies both to entry into another controller's area of jurisdiction and to direct pilot instructions that affect other controllers' areas.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph covers both the airspace-entry case and the direct-control-instruction case.

items.situation: You want to issue a speed and altitude change directly to a pilot, and the instruction will affect an adjacent controller's airspace. Another controller says it is enough to ask a flight service station to relay the instruction because that shifts the coordination burden away from you.
items.para_context: Before issuing a direct control instruction affecting another area, all required coordination must be completed with affected controllers. If the instruction is relayed through a source other than another radar controller, the issuing controller still bears responsibility for ensuring required coordination is completed.

choices.text: Ensure the affected controllers are coordinated with before the instruction is issued, even if another source relays it to the pilot.
Let the relaying source assume all coordination responsibility because the instruction is no longer being given by you directly.
Issue the instruction first and coordinate only if the affected controller later objects.
Skip coordination because heading, route, speed, and altitude changes never require it if the aircraft stays on frequency.

items.explanation: Relayed delivery does not transfer the issuing controller's coordination obligation.

items.question_text: Under what conditions is control of an aircraft transferred?

choices.text: At a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude, or at completion of a radar handoff and frequency change when authorized by an LOA or facility directive.
Whenever the aircraft is nearing a sector boundary, regardless of whether coordination conditions are met.
Only after the receiving controller issues a new clearance directly to the aircraft.
Only when the pilot requests a different sector or ARTCC.

items.explanation: Those are the listed control-transfer conditions.

items.question_text: Controllers must transfer control of an aircraft only after eliminating any potential conflict with other aircraft for which they have separation responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Potential conflicts with aircraft still under your separation responsibility are removed before control is transferred.

items.question_text: If specific coordination has not occurred and no LOA or facility directive says otherwise, a controller must assume control of an aircraft only after it is in that controller's area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Early assumption of control requires specific coordination or other specified authorization.

items.question_text: This control-transfer rule applies both to when transferring control away and to when assuming control from another controller.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph addresses conditions for both giving up and taking on control.

items.situation: A radar handoff is complete, but a potential conflict still exists with another aircraft for which you retain separation responsibility. Another controller says the handoff completion alone means control can be transferred immediately because the receiving controller now sees the target.
items.para_context: Control is transferred under the listed handoff or coordinated conditions, but only after the transferring controller has eliminated any potential conflict with aircraft for which that controller still has separation responsibility.

choices.text: Eliminate the potential conflict before transferring control, even though the radar handoff itself has been completed.
Transfer control immediately because the completed handoff automatically cancels your remaining separation responsibility.
Assume the pilot will resolve the conflict after frequency change because the transfer condition has already been met.
Delay only the frequency change while still transferring control, because conflict elimination is optional once radar contact is established.

items.explanation: The conflict-elimination condition still governs even when the handoff mechanics are otherwise complete.

items.question_text: Before issuing a clearance that would require flight within a surface area for which a nonapproach control tower has responsibility, what must be done?

choices.text: Coordinate on an individual aircraft basis with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless otherwise specified by LOA.
Obtain direct authorization from the pilot because tower coordination is optional for surface-area entry.
Transfer communications first and let the tower decide whether the clearance may be issued afterward.
Wait until the aircraft is already inside the surface area so the local controller can observe it directly.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires individual-aircraft coordination with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless an LOA says otherwise.

items.question_text: Controllers must transfer communications to the appropriate facility, if required, prior to operation within a surface area for which the tower has responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: If communication transfer is required, it happens before the operation within the tower-responsibility surface area.

items.question_text: If you are providing radar traffic advisory service to an aircraft that will enter another facility's airspace, you must coordinate with the appropriate control tower for transit authorization.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Transit authorization is coordinated with the appropriate tower in that radar-advisory-entry case.

items.question_text: This surface-area rule applies to coordination before clearances, to transit authorization when radar advisory service will enter another facility's airspace, and to communication transfer before tower-responsibility surface-area operations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph covers those three related coordination and communication responsibilities.

items.situation: You are providing radar traffic advisory service to an aircraft that will enter another facility's surface area. Another controller tells the pilot to obtain tower authorization on their own because radar advisory service does not require controller coordination.
items.para_context: The controller coordinates with the appropriate control tower for transit authorization when radar traffic advisory service will enter another facility's airspace. The pilot is not expected to obtain his or her own authorization through each area when in contact with a radar facility.

choices.text: Coordinate the transit authorization with the appropriate tower yourself rather than expecting the pilot to obtain it independently.
Tell the pilot to call each tower for authorization because radar traffic advisory service removes the controller from the process.
Skip coordination entirely because surface-area authorization is needed only for IFR aircraft.
Wait until the aircraft is inside the surface area and then ask the tower to approve the transit retroactively.

items.explanation: The pilot is not expected to self-coordinate through each area while working with a radar facility.

items.question_text: To what operations may MARSA be applied?
items.explanation: MARSA applies only to specified military operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate FAA or military document.

choices.text: Only to military operations specified in a letter of agreement or other appropriate FAA or military document.
To any operation whenever both pilots agree to it on frequency.
Only to VFR operations inside military special use airspace.
To any operation that ATC believes would simplify sector workload.

items.question_text: Who has the command prerogative for application of MARSA?
items.explanation: The note says MARSA application is a military command prerogative.

choices.text: Military command.
ATC facilities.
The lead aircraft pilot acting alone.
The scheduling agency acting without command authorization.

items.question_text: What is ATC's sole responsibility regarding the use of MARSA?
items.explanation: ATC's role is separating the MARSA operation from other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Provide separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA operations and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.
Invoke or deny MARSA on behalf of the military participants.
Assign responsibility among all participating MARSA aircraft.
Determine which military aircraft are authorized to enter special use airspace or ATCAAs.

items.question_text: ATC facilities invoke or deny MARSA based on sector conditions and controller judgment.
items.explanation: ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Who must ensure military pilots requesting special use airspace or ATCAAs have coordinated with the scheduling agency, obtained approval, and know the MARSA procedures?
items.explanation: That responsibility belongs to DoD.

choices.text: DoD.
The controlling ARTCC or approach control facility.
The scheduling agency alone.
The military pilot's wingman.

items.question_text: Why are military procedures prescribed as additions, modifications, and exceptions to FAA procedure?
items.explanation: They are prescribed when a common procedure has not been attained or to fulfill a specific requirement.

choices.text: When a common procedure has not been attained or to fulfill a specific requirement.
Whenever a military unit prefers local wording instead of FAA procedure.
Only when civil traffic is not present.
Whenever a military facility wants to ignore a standing LOA temporarily.

items.question_text: Which facilities must apply the procedures of the military service that operates them?
items.explanation: Facilities operated by that military service apply its procedures.

choices.text: ATC facilities operated by that military service.
Only FAA-operated facilities serving mixed-use airports.
Any facility whenever a military aircraft checks in first.
Only facilities specifically named in a NOTAM.

items.question_text: If a facility exclusively supports a designated military airport, what determines which military procedures apply?
items.explanation: The designation of the supported military airport determines which military procedures apply.

choices.text: The designation of the military airport being exclusively supported.
The parent organization of the facility, regardless of the airport it supports.
The first aircraft type served each day.
The nearest military unit's preferred checklist.

items.question_text: Other ATC facilities may apply military procedures when specified in a letter of agreement.
items.explanation: Subparagraph c explicitly allows that application path.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: At an FAA facility that provides approach service to both a Naval Air Station and a civil airport, what procedures does the example say are applied at both locations?
items.explanation: The example says the FAA facility applies basic FAA procedures at both locations.

choices.text: Basic FAA procedures.
USN procedures at the military field and FAA procedures at the civil field.
USAF procedures because the facility is civilian.
Any procedures chosen by the local watch supervisor for the shift.

items.question_text: How are formation flights controlled?
items.explanation: Formation flights are controlled as a single aircraft.

choices.text: As a single aircraft.
As separate aircraft at all times.
As separate aircraft until they are level at a common altitude.
As a single aircraft only when all members share the same beacon code type.

items.question_text: Who has separation responsibility between aircraft within the formation?
items.explanation: That responsibility rests with the flight leader and the pilots of the other aircraft in the formation.

choices.text: The flight leader and the pilots of the other aircraft in the formation.
ATC, because the formation is controlled as a single aircraft.
Only the trailing pilots, because the lead aircraft cannot be responsible for internal spacing.
The receiving controller after radar handoff is complete.

items.question_text: Which conditions are required for a two-aircraft formation join-up to be supported?
items.explanation: It requires a request from a participating pilot, concurrence by all participating pilots, and a report by either pilot that the other is in sight.

choices.text: A request by a participating pilot, concurrence of all participating pilots, and a report by either pilot that the other is in sight.
Any two of those three conditions.
Only a request by the lead aircraft and radar identification of both aircraft.
Only ATC concurrence and a common destination airport.

items.question_text: For a standard formation after join-up, which aircraft squawks the ATC-assigned beacon code?
items.explanation: Only the lead aircraft squawks the assigned code in a standard formation; the others squawk standby.

choices.text: Only the lead aircraft.
All aircraft in the formation.
Only the nonlead aircraft.
Whichever aircraft is closest to the fix where join-up was completed.

items.question_text: For a nonstandard formation, each aircraft should squawk an ATC-assigned beacon code, although controller discretion may allow standby squawk when operationally advantageous.
items.explanation: That is the nonstandard-formation beacon-code rule.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When a formation breaks up at FL290 or above, who must ensure all aircraft or flights receive the proper equipment suffix?
items.explanation: The controller initiating the break-up ensures proper suffix assignment on breakup.

choices.text: The controller initiating the break-up.
Only the flight leader.
The first center sector that handled the original departure.
The scheduling agency that approved the mission.

items.question_text: What must be ensured before an aircraft under your control enters another controller's area of jurisdiction?
items.explanation: The necessary coordination must be accomplished first.

choices.text: That the necessary coordination has been accomplished.
That the pilot has changed frequency, regardless of coordination.
That the receiving controller has already accepted full separation responsibility in writing.
That the aircraft is within five miles of the receiving controller's boundary.

items.question_text: Before directly issuing a control instruction that will change heading, route, speed, or altitude and affect another controller's area, what is required?
items.explanation: All affected controllers must be coordinated with unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise.

choices.text: Ensure coordination has been completed with all affected controllers unless otherwise specified by an LOA or facility directive.
Issue the instruction first and coordinate only if a controller later objects.
Only coordinate if the pilot asks who controls the next airspace.
Transfer communications first and let the next controller decide whether coordination was necessary.

items.question_text: If your instruction will be relayed to the pilot through FSS, radio, or another pilot, who still has responsibility for ensuring the required coordination is completed?
items.explanation: The issuing controller still has that responsibility.

choices.text: The controller issuing the control instruction.
The relaying source that speaks directly to the pilot.
The pilot, because the instruction is no longer direct.
The receiving controller whose airspace may be affected.

items.question_text: Ensuring all required coordination has been completed always means the controller issuing the instruction personally performs the coordination action.
items.explanation: The note says ensuring completion does not necessarily mean the issuing controller personally performs the coordination.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What does the note identify as good operating practice in unusual circumstances such as route or airspace changes?
items.explanation: Controllers should confirm that required coordination has been or will be effected.

choices.text: Confirm that required coordination has been or will be effected.
Suspend all direct pilot instructions until a supervisor completes the coordination personally.
Treat recently modified sectors as exempt from coordination until the configuration stabilizes.
Delegate all coordination to the pilot because the circumstances are unusual.

items.question_text: Which of the following is one listed way control may be transferred?
items.explanation: Control may be transferred at a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude, or under the authorized handoff-and-frequency-change method.

choices.text: At a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude.
Whenever the target is visible on both controllers' scopes, regardless of any coordinated terms.
Only after the receiving controller issues a fresh route clearance.
Only when the pilot acknowledges the new sector boundary by voice.

items.question_text: What additional authorization is required for transfer at the time a radar handoff and frequency change have been completed?
items.explanation: An LOA or facility directive must authorize that type and extent of transferred control.

choices.text: A facility directive or letter of agreement that specifies the type and extent of control transferred.
A pilot request for transfer as soon as radar contact is established.
A supervisor override whenever the traffic load is light.
A second radar handoff after the frequency change is already complete.

items.question_text: What must be true before you transfer control of an aircraft away from your position?
items.explanation: Any potential conflict with other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility must be eliminated first.

choices.text: Any potential conflict with other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility has been eliminated.
The receiving controller has verbally agreed to accept the conflict.
The pilot has changed frequency, even if the conflict still exists.
The aircraft is within ten miles of the receiving area boundary.

items.question_text: A controller may assume control of an aircraft before it enters the controller's area of jurisdiction whenever radar contact exists, even if no specific coordination or LOA provision applies.
items.explanation: Absent specific coordination or LOA/facility-directive authorization, control is assumed only after the aircraft is in your area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What does subparagraph c say about assuming control of an aircraft?
items.explanation: You assume control only after the aircraft is in your area unless specific coordination or an LOA/facility directive says otherwise.

choices.text: Assume control only after the aircraft is in your area of jurisdiction unless specifically coordinated or otherwise provided by LOA or facility directive.
Assume control as soon as the transferring controller begins the handoff process.
Assume control once the pilot checks in, regardless of the aircraft's location.
Assume control only after the aircraft lands in your area of jurisdiction.

items.question_text: Before issuing a clearance that requires flight within a surface area for which a nonapproach control tower has responsibility, what is normally required?
items.explanation: The controller coordinates on an individual aircraft basis with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless an LOA says otherwise.

choices.text: Coordinate on an individual aircraft basis with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless otherwise specified by LOA.
Let the pilot obtain authorization directly from the tower instead of coordinating.
Transfer communications first and allow the tower to decide after the aircraft enters the surface area.
Do nothing unless the flight is IFR.

items.question_text: What must be coordinated when you provide radar traffic advisory service to an aircraft that will enter another facility's airspace?
items.explanation: The controller coordinates with the appropriate control tower for transit authorization.

choices.text: Transit authorization with the appropriate control tower.
An emergency declaration if the aircraft intends to cross a Class D surface area.
Only a weather briefing for the pilot before the surface-area entry.
Only the next frequency assignment, because tower authorization is the pilot's duty.

items.question_text: When is the pilot expected to obtain separate authorization through each area while in contact with a radar facility?
items.explanation: The note says the pilot is not expected to obtain his or her own authorization through each area in that situation.

choices.text: The pilot is not expected to obtain separate authorization through each area while in contact with a radar facility.
Always, because radar advisory service does not include any controller coordination duty.
Only when entering a Class D surface area under VFR.
Only when the aircraft is receiving an IFR clearance.

items.question_text: If required, when are communications transferred to the appropriate facility for surface-area operations?
items.explanation: Communications are transferred prior to operation within the surface area for which the tower has responsibility.

choices.text: Prior to operation within the surface area.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the surface-area boundary.
At any convenient time after the tower has visually acquired the aircraft.
Only when the aircraft asks to change frequency.

items.question_text: A radar facility may expect the pilot to obtain his or her own authorization through each surface area when radar traffic advisory service is being provided.
items.explanation: The note says the pilot is not expected to obtain that authorization while in contact with a radar facility.

choices.text: True
False
